ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for tahu telur (deep-fried tofu-egg omelette). Mapped to nearest tofu-based food product class. Exact entry absent as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (tofu and soy-based dishes) is the closest defensible match. No specific code exists for tahu telur as a composite deep-fried tofu-egg dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for tahu telur as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tahu telur is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one plate comprising two to three pieces of deep-fried tofu-egg omelette with accompaniments.

nutrient_source.title: Tahu Telur — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tahu Telur (cr_id F09050015). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, tofu deep-fried, #16427) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; tofu and mixed soy-egg dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tahu telur. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tofu-based dishes and mixed egg-soy preparations. Tofu has a reported GI of approximately 15–32; the addition of sweet soy sauce (kecap manis) and egg raises the composite GI. A conservative midpoint of 50 is applied, consistent with analogous mixed soy-egg dishes in the Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (50 × 6.59 × 2.0) / 100 = 6.6 g (200 g serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium at 168 mg/100 g; sweet soy sauce may add incremental sodium
hypertension.guidance: Tahu telur contains 168 mg sodium per 100 g, which is below the 200 mg/100 g threshold for high-sodium solid foods; patients with hypertension should request reduced kecap manis and ask for peanut sauce on the side to limit cumulative sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.6 g/100 g), moderate sugar (4.9 g/100 g), high protein and fat slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 6.6 g carbohydrate and 4.9 g sugar per 100 g, and a low estimated GI of 50, tahu telur has a low glycemic load of approximately 6.6 per standard serving; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit kecap manis to reduce added sugar and monitor portion size of peanut sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (20.7 g/100 g) from deep-frying; saturated fat estimated at 3.2 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying contributes significantly to the 20.7 g total fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request a pan-fried preparation where possible, and be aware that peanut sauce adds additional fat and calories.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs); chili is a nightshade that may trigger inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tahu telur is deep-fried, generating AGEs that may worsen joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; patients should request a pan-fried or baked version and consider omitting chili to reduce potential nightshade-related inflammatory triggers.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tahu telur is a popular Indonesian and Singaporean dish consisting of firm tofu and beaten egg deep-fried together to form a crispy, golden omelette-style patty. The dish is typically served with sweet dark soy sauce (kecap manis), fresh bean sprouts, sliced chili, and sometimes peanut sauce. The exterior is crisp from deep-frying while the interior remains soft and custardy, combining the mild flavour of tofu with the richness of egg. Nutritionally, tahu telur provides 256 kcal per 100 g, with 10.8 g protein, 20.7 g fat, and only 6.6 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. The calcium content is notable at 137 mg/100 g from tofu, and dietary fibre is 3.0 g/100 g. The deep-frying method raises cardiovascular concern due to elevated fat content and generation of advanced glycation end products (AGEs). For individuals with type 2 diabetes, the low carbohydrate and low glycemic load profile is favourable, though kecap manis adds concentrated sugar and should be moderated. Sodium is moderate at 168 mg/100 g. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains soy, egg, and peanut allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tahu telur?
Is tahu telur suitable for diabetics?
What is tahu telur made of?
Is tahu telur high in protein?
Can I eat tahu telur if I have high blood pressure?
What sauce is served with tahu telur?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F09050015) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy tofu deep-fried entry as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. All other values are exact from the approved primary source.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from approved sources): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for tahu telur (F09050015) and are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific composite dish as of 2026. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for deep-fried tofu (#16427) as a proxy, given HPB does not report saturated fat for this dish; this is an adapted value. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu-based and mixed soy-egg dishes; no direct GI measurement for tahu telur exists in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 6.59 × 2.0) / 100 = 6.6 for a 200 g serving. The dish is classified as Indonesian in origin but is widely consumed in Singapore as a hawker dish. Tahu telur is not suitable for individuals with soy, egg, or peanut allergies. The high-fat warning in the original dietary_tags is retained as a clinical caution flag.
